title: Timepoint 1 — Initial admission (hospital day 5)
scenarioBrief: Marcus Bell is a previously healthy 43-year-old man admitted on 7/6/2027 with an acute, essentially unprovoked SEGMENTAL pulmonary embolism (low-risk — troponin negative, no right-heart strain) and an extensive right lower extremity DVT. He was started on anticoagulation with prompt improvement. Imaging incidentally revealed a liver abnormality, and a dedicated liver MRI now shows a 17 cm posterior-right-lobe mass with additional left-lobe foci and apparent thrombus in the right portal vein; radiology has a high suspicion for malignancy and recommends considering a needle biopsy. A triple-phase liver CT is non-diagnostic but also concerning. He is clinically well and the team is planning to transition him to apixaban and complete the workup. Review the chart (including the imaging reports) before answering.

chartGate._notes: TP1 visibility: the initial admission through HD5 — ED H&P, CTPA (segmental PE + incidental liver finding), RLE duplex (extensive DVT), TTE (no RV strain), liver MRI (17 cm mass + portal-vein thrombus + biopsy recommendation), triple-phase liver CT (non-diagnostic, concerning), admission/HD3/HD5 progress notes, the hematology consult, admission and malignancy-workup labs, and the inpatient orders (heparin infusion, transition-to-apixaban plan, outpatient biopsy referral). NOT visible: the discharge summary, the outpatient hematology note, the IR pre-procedure note that specifies the exact peri-procedural hold, and the entire readmission/recurrence (CTA, repeat TTE, etc.).

prompts.question: How long would you anticoagulate this patient before biopsying the liver lesion, and what is your rationale for choosing that amount of time?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
This question combines TWO sub-parts. Score both and sum.

SUB-PART 1a — How long to anticoagulate before biopsy (5 points):
Award 5 points for either: (a) "4 weeks as long as no DVT on recheck at 4 weeks", OR (b) "4-6 weeks". Either answer = 5/5.

SUB-PART 1b — Rationale. Apply ONE branch based on the duration the resident chose.

SHORT branch (short course, < 3 months): award 2 points each for up to SIX of the following (max 12):
a) ~50% of people have clot resolution at 4 weeks of anticoagulation
b) The anticoagulation pause for the procedure is short and the patient resumes anticoagulation soon afterward
c) Even if a solid cancer (not necessarily curable), treating early may still improve outcome (early diagnosis important)
d) Most VTE will have improved by 4-6 weeks (even if not fully resolved)
e) Even after only ~6 weeks total anticoagulation, most patients do well (despite higher recurrence risk with short duration)
f) Risk the malignancy will progress if we don't biopsy promptly
g) Risk the malignancy will have complications if we don't biopsy promptly
h) The mass could be a curable condition (e.g., lymphoma), so prompt biopsy is important

LONG branch (long course, > 3 months): award 2 points each for up to FOUR of the following (max 8):
a) Data show 6 months anticoagulation has lower recurrence than 6 weeks
b) Especially high recurrence risk in cancer-associated clot vs non-cancer clot
c) Likely not curable (likely metastatic), so less urgency to diagnose/treat the mass
d) Patient has a heavy clot burden (distinct from heavily symptomatic)

APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 5 + 12 = 17 for a short-branch answer; 5 + 8 = 13 for a long-branch answer. Use the maximum for the branch the resident actually took.

prompts.question: Thinking beyond this specific patient: which general factors would you weigh in deciding the timing of biopsy for a hepatic lesion in a patient with a new PE and DVT? Which factors would push you to biopsy PROMPTLY, and which would push you to DELAY until after a longer period of anticoagulation?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Award 2 points for each distinct factor, up to 10 points for PROMPT factors and up to 10 for DELAY factors (maximum 20 total).

Factors favoring PROMPT biopsy (2 pts each, up to 10):
a) There is a need for a biopsy (can't diagnose by imaging alone)
b) Urgency to define the lesion to start treatment
c) The patient has symptoms from the disease/mass
d) Organ compromise from the presumed mass (e.g., biliary obstruction, tumor lysis)
e) The VTE is limited to lower extremities or is a small-volume PE (lower-risk clot)
f) Concern for a potentially curable condition (e.g., lymphoma)
g) Concern for a rapidly progressive cancer where delay raises complication risk
h) Rapid progression of the disease/mass already
i) The DVT or PE is asymptomatic
j) DVT is in upper rather than lower extremities
k) Clot is distal rather than proximal lower-extremity DVT

Factors favoring DELAY of biopsy (2 pts each, up to 10):
a) Radiology had lower suspicion for malignancy / suspicion for a non-malignant condition
b) A way exists to make the diagnosis without biopsy
c) Hemodynamically substantial PE
d) Large clot burden
e) Poor cardiopulmonary reserve
f) Concern for a slow-growing (vs fast-growing) malignancy
g) Concern for metastatic non-curable disease vs a curable condition like lymphoma
h) Metastatic vs curable disease, with higher VTE-recurrence risk on short anticoagulation in high cancer burden
i) The patient has recurrent clots
j) Recurrent clots, especially after prior anticoagulation holds
k) A necessary periprocedural anticoagulation hold would be longer
l) The patient could not tolerate chemotherapy if a cancer were found

APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 20.

prompts.question: When a patient on a direct oral anticoagulant (here, apixaban) is to undergo an invasive procedure such as a liver biopsy, how long before the procedure would you hold the anticoagulant, and what is your rationale?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Award 5 points for either answer:
a) 48 hours, OR
b) 48 hours (unless chronic kidney disease or dabigatran, then possibly 72 hours).
Either answer = 5/5. APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 5.

prompts.question: When a patient on a direct oral anticoagulant is to undergo an invasive procedure, what factors would help you decide whether or not to use bridging anticoagulation?

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Award 2 points for each distinct factor the resident names, up to 10 points (any 5 = 10/10):
a) The indication for anticoagulation
b) Presence/absence of a high-risk hereditary or acquired thrombophilia (e.g., antiphospholipid syndrome, protein C/S deficiency, antithrombin III deficiency)
c) Whether the patient has had recurrent thrombosis
d) Recurrent thrombosis, especially during prior anticoagulation holds
e) Whether the clot is recent or not
f) The CHA2DS2-VASc score (only in patients with atrial fibrillation)
g) Duration of anticoagulation already given for the current VTE
h) Provoked vs unprovoked nature of the VTE
i) The procedural bleeding risk
j) The compressibility of the procedure site
k) The danger of bleeding at that particular biopsy site
l) Presence/suspicion of malignancy (higher clotting risk off anticoagulation)
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 10.
